1: "Go up and down the streets of Jerusalem, look around and consider; search through her public squares to see whether you can find a man — anyone who does justice and seeks truth, so that I may forgive her" — the one-righteous-person search: the direct parallel to Abraham's intercession for Sodom (Genesis 18:23-33) where YHWH offered to spare the city for ten righteous. Here the standard has been reduced to one — and the search fails. The verb suru (go about, search systematically, H7751) describes a thorough investigation of the city's streets (chutzot) and squares (rehovot). The failure to find even one person who does justice (oseh mishpat) and seeks truth (mevaqesh emunah) is the formal legal finding that makes forgiveness impossible: the city has fallen below the minimum standard for covenant clemency. It has fallen below Sodom's standard.
2: "Even when they say 'As the LORD lives,' they are swearing falsely" — the false-oath indictment: the standard Israelite oath formula (chai YHWH) has been emptied of content. The covenant formula is retained while the covenant reality (truth, justice, faithfulness) is abandoned. This is the worship-without-substance critique that runs through the prophets: Isaiah 29:13 ("this people honors me with their lips but their heart is far from me"); Amos 5:21-24 (YHWH despises feasts while justice rolls down like water). The false oath is particularly serious because swearing by YHWH's name in falsehood is a direct violation of the third commandment (Exodus 20:7; Deuteronomy 5:11).
3: "LORD, do not your eyes look for faithfulness? You struck them, but they felt nothing; you crushed them, but they refused to take correction; they have made their faces harder than rock; they refuse to return" — the divine-discipline failure: YHWH struck (hikita) and crushed (kiletam) Israel repeatedly through historical judgments, but there was no corrective response. The hardened face (chizqu faneihem misela) is the metaphor for the obstinacy that makes all discipline futile. Proverbs uses the hardened-face image for the shameless person (Jeremiah 21:29: "a wicked man puts on a bold face"). The refusal to return (me'enu lashuvah) names exactly the failure of v1's repentance call — Israel has been struck and has refused the shuv-movement toward YHWH.
4: "Then I thought, 'These are only the poor; they are ignorant; they do not know the way of the LORD, the requirements of their God'" — Jeremiah's first explanation for the search's failure: the poor (the common people) may not know the covenant requirements. The dalim (H1800, the poor, the lowly) lack access to the instruction that the educated classes receive. This is a charitable hypothesis — perhaps the leaders know better. The distinction between the knowing elite and the ignorant poor is a common ANE social stratification assumption; Jeremiah momentarily applies it before discovering (v5) that it is false. Both classes have equally refused the covenant.
5: "So I went to the leaders and spoke to them, for they surely know the way of the LORD, the requirements of their God. But with one accord they have broken off the yoke and torn off the bonds" — the disproof of the charitable hypothesis: the leaders (gedolim, H1419 — the great ones, the ruling class) know perfectly well what the covenant requires and have deliberately refused it. The yoke-breaking metaphor (shaveru ol qitzzu mosherot) is from the suzerainty-treaty context: the yoke is the vassal's submission to the suzerain. Breaking the yoke means refusing covenant-submission to YHWH. Jeremiah 2:20 uses the same yoke-breaking metaphor for Israel's historical apostasy: "long ago you broke off your yoke and tore off your bonds." The leaders, who are responsible for covenant instruction, are the worst violators.
6: "Therefore a lion from the forest will attack them, a wolf from the desert will ravage them, a leopard will lurk near their cities" — the three-predator curse: lion (aryeh, H738), wolf (ze'ev, H2061), and leopard (namer, H5246) represent three different predation strategies — the lion attacks openly with power, the wolf raids in packs from the wilderness, the leopard lurks and ambushes near settled areas. Together they cover all attack-vectors. The same three predators appear in Hosea 13:7-8 (YHWH himself will be like a lion, leopard, and bear against Israel). The three animals also match the three scales of Judah's apostasy: the leaders (lion-bold), the masses (wolf-pack), and the hidden sinners (leopard-lurkers).
7: "How can I forgive you for this? Your children have abandoned me and sworn oaths by gods that are no gods at all. When I fed them to the full, they committed adultery and trooped to the house of a prostitute" — YHWH's rhetorical question about the impossibility of forgiveness: ech eslach-lach (how shall I forgive you for this?). The grounds given: (1) oath-swearing by non-gods; (2) adultery after satiation — the prosperity of the land produced apostasy rather than gratitude. The connection between full feeding and unfaithfulness echoes Deuteronomy 6:11-12 ("eat and be full... take care lest you forget") and Jeremiah 8:12-14 ("when you have eaten and are full... your heart will be lifted up and you will forget"). Satiation produces the spiritual amnesia that produces apostasy.
8: "They are like well-fed, lusty stallions — each neighing after another man's wife" — the stallion metaphor for sexual insatiability: the well-fed horse (susim mezunim, fattened horses, H2109) neigh after each other's mares regardless of ownership. The prophets consistently use marriage and sexual fidelity as the central metaphor for the covenant relationship (Hos 1-3; Ezek 16,23; Jeremiah 2:20-25). The specific choice of the stallion (a symbol of unchecked appetite and raw drive) emphasizes that this is not a failure of knowledge but a failure of will — they know what they want and pursue it regardless of covenant-constraint.
9: "Should I not call them to account for these things? declares the LORD. Should I not take vengeance on a nation like this?" — the divine-accountability refrain: the rhetorical question with the expected answer "yes" recurs verbatim at v29 and Jeremiah 9:9, forming a structural bracket around the corruption-indictment sections of Jeremiah 5-9. The combination of paqad (H6485, visit, call to account, punish) and naqam (H5358, take vengeance) establishes that YHWH's response to Israel's covenant-breach is both juridical (calling to account) and retributive (vengeance) — the two aspects of covenant-enforcement that the Deuteronomy curse-structure anticipated.
10: "Advance through her vineyards and ravage them, but do not destroy them completely; strip off her branches, for they do not belong to the LORD" — the pruning metaphor for partial judgment: YHWH commands the invasion to strip the vine without total destruction. The vine was Israel's identity symbol (Psalms 80:8-16; Isaiah 5:1-7; Jeremiah 2:21) — YHWH is the vinedresser, Israel the vine. The qualification "but do not destroy them completely" (velo tassu kalah) repeats Jeremiah 4:27's restraint — the judgment is severe but not annihilating. The final clause "for they do not belong to the LORD" (ki lo laYHWH hemma) is the bitter assessment: the vine, which was planted by YHWH (Jeremiah 2:21: "I planted you a choice vine"), has become a vine that does not belong to him.
11: "For the house of Israel and the house of Judah have acted completely faithlessly toward me, declares the LORD" — the comprehensive covenant-breach indictment: both the northern kingdom (Israel) and the southern (Judah) are guilty. By Jeremiah's time, the northern kingdom had already fallen (722 BCE), but Jeremiah addresses both houses — partly as a historical-theological summary and partly because Judah has not learned from Israel's fate (Jeremiah 3:6-11: Judah observed Israel's punishment and did not repent). The adverb bagod bagadu bi (acting very treacherously against me) uses the infinitive absolute for maximum emphasis: completely, thoroughly faithless.
12: "They have lied about the LORD and said, 'He will do nothing; no disaster will come upon us; we will not face sword or famine'" — the functional atheism of covenant-breaking Israel: not formal denial of YHWH's existence but denial of his active covenant-enforcement. "He will do nothing" (lo ya'aseh) is the attitude that makes the prophetic warning seem absurd — the past has delivered prosperity and peace despite covenant-violation, so why should the future be different? This is the complacency that the prophets consistently attacked (Amos 6:3: "you put off the evil day"; Isaiah 22:13: "eat and drink, for tomorrow we die"). The denial of sword and famine — exactly the covenant curses of Deut 28 — shows that the people had mentally disconnected covenant-stipulations from historical consequence.
13: "The prophets are nothing but wind; the word of God is not in them. What they threatened will now happen to them" — the people's dismissal of the true prophets: the prophets (including Jeremiah) are called ruach (H7307, wind/breath) — empty air, substance-less. The people's counter-evaluation is precisely the opposite of the truth: Jeremiah's word is YHWH's fire (v14), while the false prophets' word is wind (Ezekiel 13:3: "foolish prophets who follow their own spirit, and have seen nothing"). The reversal in v13b — "what they threatened will now happen to them" — can be read either as YHWH's response (the people's words will come back on them) or as the people's taunt (the doom-prophets will experience the doom they announced).
14: "Therefore, this is what the LORD God of Armies says: Because you have spoken like this, I will make my words in your mouth fire, and this people the wood it consumes" — the prophetic word as fire: YHWH responds to the "wind" dismissal by declaring that Jeremiah's words will prove to be fire, not wind. The fire-word metaphor is developed later in Jeremiah 23:29 ("is not my word like fire?... and like a hammer that breaks the rock in pieces?"). The title "LORD God of Armies" (YHWH Elohei Tzvaot) is a title that emphasizes YHWH's military sovereignty — it appears when YHWH acts in judgment-warfare contexts. Jeremiah himself is the flint from which the fire-word strikes; the people are the combustible material that will be consumed.
15: "Listen: I am bringing a nation against you from far away, O house of Israel, declares the LORD. It is a powerful nation, an ancient nation, a nation whose language you do not know, nor can you understand what they say" — the Foe from the North described: powerful (am ethan, H386, enduring/permanent), ancient (am me'olam), and linguistically incomprehensible to Israel. The language barrier was a significant element of ancient warfare's terror: the conquering army's commands cannot be understood, making resistance and negotiation both impossible. The Assyrian invasions employed exactly this terror — Sennacherib's field commander addressed the people of Jerusalem in Hebrew specifically to bypass this barrier (2 Kings 18:26-28).
16: "Their quivers are like open graves; they are all mighty men" — the archer imagery: the quiver (ashpato, H827) as an open grave from which death emerges with each arrow drawn. The arrow from a quiver is as certain in its deadly outcome as the grave — and the grave is always open, always receiving. The "mighty men" (giborim, H1368 — warriors, fighting men) are all skilled soldiers, not conscripted civilians: Israel will face professional soldiers, not levies.
17: "They will devour your harvests and your food — what your sons and daughters depend on for life. They will devour your flocks and your herds; they will devour your vines and your fig trees. They will pulverize with the sword your fortified cities in which you trust" — the systematic economic destruction: the enemy consumes the entire economic structure. The five objects of consumption (harvest, flocks, herds, vines, fig trees) are the five pillars of Judah's agricultural economy. The final blow: the fortified cities in which Israel trusted will be smashed. The trust in fortified cities is a recurring target of prophetic critique (Isaiah 22:11; Jeremiah 4:29; Habakkuk 2:9-11) — the physical security-structure replaces trust in YHWH.
18: "Yet even in those days, declares the LORD, I will not make a complete end of you" — the restraining mercy clause repeated (cf. Jeremiah 4:27): at the maximum point of judgment description, YHWH inserts the promise of non-annihilation. The "even in those days" (vegam bayamim hahem) acknowledges the severity of what has been described while holding open the covenant-thread. This is the structural basis for Jeremiah's restoration oracles (Jeremiah 30-33): because YHWH does not make a complete end, there is a future beyond the Babylonian exile.
19: "When they ask, 'Why has the LORD our God done all this to us?' tell them, 'As you abandoned me and served foreign gods in your own land, so you will serve strangers in a land not your own'" — the covenant-explanation formula (cf. Deuteronomy 29:24-25): the nations, and eventually Israel itself, will ask the theodicy question about the catastrophe. The answer is the covenant-breach formula: you served foreign gods in your land; you will serve foreigners in their land. The symmetry is precise: the service (oved) that should have been given to YHWH alone was given to foreign gods; the punishment is service to foreign masters in foreign territory. The Babylonian exile is YHWH's covenant-mirror: what Israel did to YHWH, YHWH does to Israel.
20: "Announce this to the house of Jacob; proclaim it in Judah, saying" — the announcement formula introducing the creation-wisdom passage: the double audience (house of Jacob = the covenant community, Judah = the political entity) receives the cosmic-wisdom indictment of vv21-24. The proclamation formula (hagidu... vehashmi'u) is the herald's introduction, signaling that what follows is a formal divine declaration of public importance.
21: "Listen to this, you foolish and senseless people — you have eyes but do not see; you have ears but do not hear" — the blindness-deafness indictment quoted by Jesus: Isaiah 6:9-10 formulated the classic blindness-deafness verdict on Israel ("make their hearts dull, their ears heavy, their eyes blind"); Jeremiah 5:21 applies it again in the pre-exilic period. Jesus quotes the Isaiah version in the parable of the sower (Mark 4:12) and in explaining why he speaks in parables. Paul quotes Deuteronomy 29:4 and Isaiah 29:10 in Romans 11:8. The accusation is not merely perceptual failure but the willful refusal to perceive — the eyes and ears are working; what is missing is the heart's response to what they perceive.
22: "Should you not fear me? declares the LORD. Should you not tremble before me? I am the one who set the sand as the boundary for the sea" — the creation-wisdom argument for covenant-fear: YHWH set the sand as the sea's permanent boundary (chol shamti gvul layam chuqat olam, sand I set as the boundary for the sea, a permanent ordinance). The sand holds back the ocean not by its inherent strength but by YHWH's decree — a constant display of sovereign power that Israel observes daily along the Mediterranean coast. If the sea obeys YHWH's boundary ordinance, why does Israel not obey its covenant ordinances? The implicit argument: creation's elements know their boundaries; covenant-Israel does not know its.
23: "But this people has a stubborn, rebellious heart; they have turned away and left" — the contrast with creation's obedience: the sea stays within its boundary; Israel has a stubborn (sorer, H5637) and rebellious (umorah, H4784) heart. The same root used in the "rebellious son" law of Deuteronomy 21:18-21 — Israel is the covenant son who has become the unmanageable rebel. The "turned away and left" (yisuru vayelechu) mirrors the covenant-departure language of Deuteronomy 9:12 ("they have turned away quickly from the way I commanded"). The physical picture: the sea that stays within its boundary versus the people who turn and walk away from theirs.
24: "They do not even say in their hearts, 'Let us now fear the LORD our God, who gives the autumn and spring rains in season, who assures us of the regular weeks of harvest'" — the rain-provision argument: YHWH gives both the yoreh (H3138, early rains, autumn rains that begin the agricultural year, October-November) and the malqosh (H4456, late rains, spring rains that swell the grain before harvest, March-April). Israel benefits from this two-season rain pattern every year but does not draw the covenant-fear conclusion: YHWH controls our food supply, therefore we should fear and obey him. The seasonal agricultural rhythm is YHWH's covenant sign of provision — its regularity should produce gratitude and fear, but instead it produces presumption.
25: "Your iniquities have diverted these good things, and your sins have kept them from you" — the covenant-mechanism stated: sin diverts the good things that YHWH's natural provision would otherwise deliver. The drought and agricultural failure that Jeremiah's generation was experiencing (Jeremiah 3:3: "the autumn rains have been withheld; the spring showers have not come") were the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:23-24 (bronze sky, iron ground, rain turned to dust) made historically real. The connection between covenant-breach and agricultural failure is not mechanistic magic but YHWH's active covenant-enforcement through the creation systems he governs.
26: "For among my people there are wicked men — they lurk like hunters who set traps; they lay snares and catch people" — the economic exploitation described in hunter-trapper imagery: the wicked (resha'im) are bird-trappers who lie in ambush (yashuru shachim). The comparison of economic exploitation to animal-trapping is unusual in the OT prophets — Amos uses it differently (Jeremiah 2:6-7: selling the poor for a pair of sandals) while Jeremiah specifies the predatory technique. The trap-imagery suggests premeditation: they study their victims before acting, lay the trap carefully, and wait for the vulnerable to fall in.
27: "As a cage is full of birds, so their houses are full of goods gotten by deceit; that is how they became great and rich" — the bird-cage metaphor: the trap-laid birds fill a cage; the predatory wealth extracted by exploitation fills the houses of the wicked. "How they became great and rich" (shemenu gam ashiru) is bitter irony: their socio-economic advancement is the direct result of their exploitation of the vulnerable. This is the inverse of the covenant's blessing-structure (Deuteronomy 28:11-12): covenant-obedience produces wealth through YHWH's blessing; these men produce wealth through covenant-violation at the expense of others.
28: "They have grown fat and sleek; they surpass all limits in their evil deeds. They do not defend the rights of the fatherless, so as to make their cause prosper, and they do not uphold the justice of the poor" — the wealthy-oppressor profile: fat (shamanu, H8080) and sleek (atsemu) describes the well-fed, physically powerful oppressor. The specific failures: not defending the rights of the orphan (din yatom lo danu) and not upholding justice for the poor (mishpat evyonim lo shafetu). These two groups are Deuteronomy's most protected legal categories (Deuteronomy 10:18; Jeremiah 14:29; Jeremiah 16:11,14; Jeremiah 24:17,19-21; Jeremiah 27:19). The failure to protect them is the most direct violation of the covenant's social-justice requirements, and it is precisely what prosperity has produced in the leadership class.
29: "Should I not call them to account for these things? declares the LORD. Should I not take vengeance on a nation like this?" — the accountability refrain restated (cf. v9): the rhetorical question brackets the entire corruption-description of vv10-28. The repetition creates a formal legal structure — the charges were laid (v9), the evidence presented (vv10-28), and the verdict restated (v29). The "nation like this" (am kazeh) frames Israel as an instance of a type — a nation that has so corrupted its covenant-character that it has become unrecognizable as a covenant-people.
30: "A horrible and shocking thing has occurred in the land" — the horror-desolation formula: shamah venasha'ra niheitah (a horrible/appalling and disgusting thing). The word pair shamah (H8047, desolation/horror) and nasha'rah (H8186, something appalling/disgusting) appears also in Jeremiah 23:14 (concerning the prophets of Jerusalem), Hosea 6:10 (concerning the house of Israel), and Jeremiah 18:13 (concerning virgin Israel). The full content of the horrible thing is given in v31 — it is not merely a moral failure but a social conspiracy between corrupt religious leaders and a willing people.
31: "The prophets prophesy what is false, the priests exercise authority on their own terms, and my people love it this way. But what will you do when the end comes?" — the systemic religious conspiracy: three groups are implicated. (1) Prophets who prophesy falsely (hannevi'im nib'u vasheqer); (2) priests who rule by their own authority (vehakohanim yirdu al yadeihem); (3) the people who love it (va'ami ahavu chen). The third element is the most damning: the false-prophets and corrupt-priests could not maintain their positions without a congregation that preferred comfortable lies to uncomfortable truth. Isaiah 30:10 makes the same point: "Say to the seers, 'Do not see,' and to the prophets, 'Do not prophesy truth to us; speak smooth things to us'" — the audience is complicit in its own deception. The closing question "what will you do when the end comes?" is the eschatological wake-up call that all three parties ignore.
